title: Backup & Recovery
concept: Snapshotting a vector index and restoring it, and why re-embedding from scratch is the fallback of last resort.

exercises.prompt: Welcome to Unit 443! Let's start with a quick concept check: What is the primary purpose of a vector database?

options.text: Measuring semantic similarity using distance metrics.
Compressing image files into JPEG format.

exercises.explain: In vector databases, distance metrics like cosine similarity or Euclidean distance are used to measure how closely related two vector embeddings are in the vector space.

cards.title: The Cost of Starting Over
cards.body: If your database crashes, recalculating millions of embeddings using OpenAI's API would cost thousands of dollars and take hours. We need a way to back up the pre-calculated vectors directly!

cards.title: What is a Vector Snapshot?
cards.body: A snapshot is a point-in-time copy of your vector database's index configuration and vector data. Restoring it recovers your index structure without running raw text back through an embedding model.

cards.title: The Game Save Analogy
cards.body: Think of snapshotting like saving your progress in a giant open-world video game. Instead of replaying the entire game from the beginning, you instantly load the exact world state and player location from your save file.

cards.title: Real-World Recovery
cards.body: In Pinecone or Milvus, instead of paying for API calls to re-embed 10 million product descriptions after a corrupt update, you trigger a restore command from your weekly snapshot bucket to recover the index in minutes.

cards.title: The 'Re-embed Everything' Trap
cards.body: Many believe they can just rely on raw text backups and re-generate embeddings if disaster strikes. While true, this is the slowest, most expensive option and should only be your ultimate safety net.

cards.title: Restore vs. Re-embed
cards.body: Compare the speed and cost of restoring an index snapshot versus regenerating embeddings from raw data.

left.text: Instant recovery, zero API costs, loads pre-calculated vector index state.

right.text: High API costs, hours of GPU time, recalculates every vector from text.

exercises.prompt: How does restoring a vector index snapshot save time and API costs?

options.text: It runs all raw text through the embedding model again automatically.
It restores pre-computed vectors and index states without re-calculating embeddings.
It only backs up the metadata, discarding the vectors.
It converts high-dimensional vectors back into raw English text.

exercises.explain: A snapshot captures the pre-computed vectors and the index structure, meaning you don't need to call any LLM or embedding model APIs to rebuild it.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A vector database snapshot captures both the vector embeddings and the index configuration.
exercises.explain: A snapshot freezes the exact state of the vectors and index at a specific moment, allowing you to restore to that exact state later.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept about rebuilding vector databases from raw text:
exercises.sentence: Because re-generating millions of embeddings is slow and expensive, it is treated as a fallback of last resort.
exercises.explain: Re-indexing from scratch is incredibly slow and expensive, making it the fallback of last resort when snapshots are unavailable.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes an AI system engineer performing a vector backup operation?

options.text: "To prevent data loss, we triggered a point-in-time snapshot of the HNSW index and exported the serialized segments to cold storage."
"We searched the vector database using a cosine similarity threshold of 0.85 and filtered the results by the 'backup' tag."
"I converted all raw text paragraphs into 1536-dimensional vectors using the text-embedding-3-small model."

exercises.explain: Option B describes a normal metadata query, and Option C describes initial index creation. Option A uses typical system-level backup language to describe creating a point-in-time image of the vector index.

exercises.prompt: Your vector database hosting millions of high-dimensional vectors crashes. Why is restoring a index snapshot heavily preferred over re-embedding the source documents from scratch?

options.text: Re-embedding is faster but costs more in storage fees.
Re-embedding requires high GPU/API costs and time, while restoring a snapshot is fast and cheap.
Snapshots are only useful if the embedding model changes.
Vector databases automatically re-embed data for free during crashes.

exercises.explain: Re-embedding millions of documents requires massive API costs and GPU hours, whereas restoring from a snapshot is nearly instant and computationally cheap. Therefore, re-embedding is the costly fallback of last resort.
